results.title: Fossils Reveal a Possible New Tyrannosaur Species, the Closest Relative of T. Rex
results.content:
Historically, however, the subject of tyrannosaur species has been controversial among scientists. A 2022 paper, for example, suggested that T. rex should be split into three separate species—and this proposal was so disputed that another group of researchers published a rebuttal paper months later. Other experts have spent decades debating whether a certain group of fossils represent T. rex juveniles or another dinosaur entirely.

Similarly, the new paper hasn’t convinced everyone. “I hesitate to regard Tyrannosaurus mcraeensis as being distinct from Tyrannosaurus rex,” Jared Voris, who studies tyrannosaur biology at the University of Calgary in Canada and did not contribute to the findings, tells New Scientist’s Riley Black.

A close-up picture of a dinosaur jaw with a few teeth/ [...] People boating in New Mexico’s Hall Lake Formation found the prehistoric predator’s jaw in 1983, Spencer Lucas, a co-author of the study and paleontologist at the New Mexico Museum of Natural History and Science, tells ABC News’ Julia Jacobo.

Paleontologists later uncovered more of its skull and classified the creature as T. rex. But the shape of its bones, their age and where they were found all point to the dinosaur being of a different species, say the researchers. They have named this creature Tyrannosaurus mcraeensis, after the McRae Formation where it was discovered.

“If a T. rex saw this dinosaur, the one we’ve just named, it would have known it wasn’t dealing with a T. rex,” Lucas says to Scientific American’s Meghan Bartels. [...] T. rex was an apex predator in its time, living between 66 and 68 million years ago in what is now the western United States. The carnivorous dinosaurs stood 12 feet tall and 40 feet long, and they weighed up to eight tons.

But despite the popular interest in these fierce predators, scientists are unsure where T. rex originated. Paleontologists haven’t dug up any of its close relatives in North America from the time before the species appeared, the study authors write. But T. mcraeensis, as a potential relative of T. rex, could shed light on this question.

results.title: Types of Tyrannosaur Dinosaurs and the Origins of T. rex
results.content:
### Tyrannosaurus rex — Tyrant Lizard King — Laramidia — 67 to 66 Mya

Tyrannosaurus rex dinosaurs on display at the Carnegie Museum of Natural History

Reaching lengths of more than 40 feet, Tyrannosaurus rex was the largest and most famous of the tyrannosaurs. It represents the peak of the giant tyrannosaur body plan: a massive skull, bone-crushing jaws, forward-facing eyes, powerful hind limbs, and reduced but muscular arms.

T. rex lived across Laramidia near the very end of the Cretaceous. Fossils are found from Canada through the western United States, including famous formations such as the Hell Creek and Lance formations. For a complete guide, visit the main Tyrannosaurus rex page.

### Nanuqsaurus hoglundi — Polar Bear Lizard — Polar Laramidia — 69 Mya [...] At about 80 million years old, Lythronax argestes is one of the oldest known large tyrannosaurids. Its fossils come from Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument in Utah, which was part of southern Laramidia during the Late Cretaceous.

Although smaller than T. rex, Lythronax already had many classic tyrannosaur traits, including a short, wide skull, robust teeth, forward-facing eyes, and reduced arms. This shows that several important tyrannosaur features evolved earlier than the final appearance of Tyrannosaurus rex.

### Daspletosaurus torosus — Muscular Frightful Lizard — North Laramidia — 76 Mya

Reconstructed Daspletosaurus dinosaur specimen FMNH PR308 from Alberta [...] Group: Theropod dinosaurs within Tyrannosauroidea and Tyrannosauridae.
  
  
Best-known member: Tyrannosaurus rex, the giant Late Cretaceous predator from western North America.
  
  
Distribution: Mainly western North America (Laramidia) and Asia, with earlier tyrannosauroids known more broadly across Laurasia.
  
  
Time range: Early tyrannosauroids appeared long before T. rex, while the largest tyrannosaurids dominated near the end of the Cretaceous.
  
  
Body size: Early forms were relatively small, while later forms such as T. rex and Tarbosaurus reached giant size.
  
  
Major traits: Large skulls, strong jaws, forward-facing eyes, powerful hind limbs, and reduced arms in the later giant forms.

results.title: Scientists thought this fossil was a teen T. rex. Turns out it's a new tyrannosaur : NPR
results.content:
The majority of paleontologists, after some debate, had dismissed the idea that any other tyrannosaurs existed besides T. rex in what is now western North America. And yet, the sands of Montana seem to have disgorged a second one. In a broader analysis of other fossils, the two researchers also uncovered what they say is a third tyrannosaur species, formerly presumed to be a juvenile T. rex.

"We knew the specimen was exceptional," says Zanno. "We did not realize it would turn decades of research about T. rex on its head."

### "Just insanely good"

The Montana dig began on a breezy summer day when Phipps' friend, Mark Eatman, found an exposed pelvis and femur at the bottom of a canyon. Phipps thought maybe there was half a dinosaur hidden below. [...] In addition, while making models of all those other alleged T. rex skeletons, Zanno says they identified another new species of tyrannosaur, one they're calling Nanotyrannus lethaeus. "The name comes from Greco-Roman mythology for the [Lethe] River where souls used to drink and forget their prior lives and be reincarnated," she says. "That's a reference to this animal having been under our nose and not recognized for what it was."

For years, most researchers believed there was just one tyrannosaur in this part of North America — rex. But now the king has been de-throned. There were at least two other tyrannosaurs there holding court. [...] That discovery in 2006 now appears to have overturned decades of dinosaur dogma about Tyrannosaurus rex, the fearsome giant long thought to be the sole top predator stalking the late Cretaceous in what is now western North America. In a paper in the journal Nature, paleontologists Lindsay Zanno and James Napoli conclude that some of the bones from that specimen belong not to a teenage T. rex, but to a fully grown individual of a different tyrannosaur species — Nanotyrannus lancensis.

This fossil of a mammal biting a dinosaur captures a death battle's final moments

results.title: 7 Dinosaurs With Horns Like A Rhino (One Looks Like a T-Rex with a Horn!) - A-Z Animals
results.content:
The Aquiops americanus is a small dinosaur around the size of a cat. This species lived 109 million years ago in North America, and it shows how different Ceratopsian dinosaurs could be. This dinosaur had a curved beak with a small horn on the tip of it. The Aquilops is only estimated to have grown to around 2 feet (0.6 meters) and weighed a very light 3 lbs (1.3 kgs).

Compared to other ceratopsian dinosaurs the Aquiolps was one of the smallest, and also lacked the very large horns and frill. Scientists believed this species survived off a diet of fruit and insects. The fossil of this dinosaur was first discovered in 1997 and was only recently described in 2014.

## 4. Monoclonius

Monoclonius - Horned Dinosaur

The name of the Monoclonius is in reference to the single horn on its face [...] The Bagoceratops had a small ridge-like horn on its face.

Bagoceratops lived in Asia during the Cretaceous period. This dinosaur is a type of Ceratopsian but is much smaller than its relatives. Bagoceratops reached around 3.3 feet long (1 meter), and weighed around 50 lbs (22 kgs). While smaller in size than other Ceratopsians, they had much smaller horns and frills. The horn of the Bagoceratops is located on its nose, and only slightly protrudes from its face.

This dinosaur was a herbivore, and its beak made it easy to tear apart plants. They walked on all fours and resembled a mini version of the larger Ceratospains. The Bagoceratops was discovered in the 1970s in Mongolia, and most of its fossils are incomplete.

## 6. Ajkaceratops

Ajkaceratops - Dinosaurs with Horns [...] Standing at a height of around 5.9 feet (1.7 meters), the Styracosaurus reaches a length of around 18 feet (5.4 meters). Only weighing around 6,000 lbs (2,721.54 kg), the large groups they traveled in increased their survival rate. Their small size makes them easier for predators to pick off when alone. A large frill was located on top of this dinosaur’s head, as well as long horns protruding out of their skull. A horn is also located on their nose, all of which are useful in defending themselves from predators.

## 3. Aquilops

The Aquilops name translates to “eagle face” as they have prominent bird-like features.
